edges.description: Impaired Notch signaling disrupts intrahepatic bile duct development.
Impaired Notch signaling perturbs cardiac and vascular morphogenesis.
Bile duct paucity causes impaired bile flow and chronic cholestasis.

edges.target: Reduced number of intrahepatic bile ducts

edges.description: Chronic cholestasis drives severe itching and symptomatic burden.

nodes.description: Developmental failure of normal intrahepatic bile duct formation produces paucity of interlobular bile ducts, the core hepatic lesion of Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Alagille syndrome (AGS) was described more than 35 years ago as a genetic entity characterised by five major features: chronic cholestasis owing to paucity of interlobular bile ducts

nodes.description: Atrial septal defects occur occasionally as part of the congenital heart disease spectrum in Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001631 | Atrial septal defect | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Vertebral segmentation abnormalities, especially butterfly vertebrae, are a classic skeletal manifestation of Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Alagille syndrome (AGS) was described more than 35 years ago as a genetic entity characterised by five major features: chronic cholestasis owing to paucity of interlobular bile ducts; peripheral pulmonary stenosis; butterfly like vertebral arch defect

nodes.description: Notch pathway dysfunction disrupts embryonic cardiovascular development, contributing to pulmonary artery stenosis and other congenital cardiac and vascular abnormalities.

evidence.snippet: Liver disease is a major cause of morbidity in this population, whereas cardiac and vascular involvement accounts for most of the mortality.

nodes.description: Chronic cholestatic liver disease is a cardinal manifestation of Alagille syndrome and usually reflects paucity of interlobular bile ducts.

evidence.snippet: Alagille syndrome (AGS) was described more than 35 years ago as a genetic entity characterised by five major features: chronic cholestasis owing to paucity of interlobular bile ducts
HP:0001396 | Cholestasis | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Bile duct paucity results in chronic cholestatic liver disease, producing jaundice, pruritus, fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, and progressive hepatic morbidity in severe cases.

evidence.reference_title: Alagille Syndrome: Current Understanding of Pathogenesis, and Challenges in Diagnosis and Management.
evidence.snippet: Alagille syndrome (ALGS) is a complex heterogenous disease with a wide array of clinical manifestations in association with cholestatic liver disease.

nodes.description: Orphanet lists corneal dystrophy (HP:0001131) as very frequent in Alagille syndrome, but clinical literature consistently identifies posterior embryotoxon (separately modeled as diagnostic) and other anterior segment changes as the characteristic ocular findings. Notably, Orphanet does not list HP:0000627 (posterior embryotoxon) in its phenotype table, suggesting the VERY_FREQUENT annotation may reflect miscoding of anterior segment features under HP:0001131. The Orphanet frequency annotation is retained here at partial support pending clinical literature confirmation.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001131 | Corneal dystrophy | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Cryptorchidism occurs occasionally in male patients with Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000028 | Cryptorchidism | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Delayed puberty occurs occasionally, likely related to chronic cholestatic malnutrition and growth impairment.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000823 | Delayed puberty | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Failure to thrive is very frequent in Alagille syndrome, reflecting cholestatic malabsorption and fat-soluble vitamin deficiency.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001508 | Failure to thrive | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Frontal bossing is a frequent craniofacial feature of Alagille syndrome, contributing to the characteristic prominent forehead.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002007 | Frontal bossing | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Hepatomegaly is very frequent in Alagille syndrome, reflecting chronic cholestatic liver disease.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002240 | Hepatomegaly | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Hypertension occurs occasionally in Alagille syndrome, potentially related to renal involvement.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000822 | Hypertension | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Intrauterine growth retardation is frequent, reflecting prenatal impact of the developmental disorder.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001511 | Intrauterine growth retardation | Frequent (79-30%)

meta.association: Causal haploinsufficiency or pathogenic heterozygous variant

evidence.snippet: A majority of patients have pathogenic mutations in either the JAG1 gene, encoding a Notch pathway ligand, or the receptor NOTCH2.

nodes.description: Most Alagille syndrome cases are caused by heterozygous pathogenic variants in JAG1 and a minority by NOTCH2 variants, establishing a developmental haploinsufficiency disorder of the Notch pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Alagille Syndrome: A Focused Review on Clinical Features, Genetics, and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Alagille syndrome (ALGS) is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by pathogenic variants in JAG1 or NOTCH2, which encode fundamental components of the Notch signaling pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Alagille syndrome is caused by mutations in human Jagged1, which encodes a ligand for Notch1.
evidence.snippet: We demonstrate four distinct coding mutations in JAG1 from four Alagille syndrome families, providing evidence that it is the causal gene for Alagille syndrome.

nodes.description: Liver transplantation is used for severe hepatic disease, growth failure, portal hypertension, or refractory pruritus.

evidence.reference_title: Alagille Syndrome: A Focused Review on Clinical Features, Genetics, and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: liver transplant indicated for severe pruritus, liver synthetic dysfunction, portal hypertension, bone fractures, and/or growth failure.

nodes.description: Long nose is a frequent facial feature contributing to the characteristic facies of Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0003189 | Long nose | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Ileal bile acid transporter inhibition with maralixibat is used to reduce cholestatic pruritus in Alagille syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Alagille Syndrome: A Focused Review on Clinical Features, Genetics, and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: New therapeutic approaches are under investigation, including ileal bile acid transporter (IBAT) inhibitors

nodes.description: Mild intellectual disability occurs occasionally in Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001256 | Intellectual disability, mild | Occasional (29-5%)
A majority of patients have pathogenic mutations in either the JAG1 gene, encoding a Notch pathway ligand, or the receptor NOTCH2.
NOTCH2 | HGNC:7882 | Alagille syndrome | MONDO:0007318 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: Characteristic facies are a classic craniofacial component of Alagille syndrome, including coarse facial features, pointed chin, round face, frontal bossing, and long nose.

evidence.snippet: Alagille syndrome (AGS) was described more than 35 years ago as a genetic entity characterised by five major features: chronic cholestasis owing to paucity of interlobular bile ducts; peripheral pulmonary stenosis; butterfly like vertebral arch defect; posterior embryotoxon and peculiar facies.
HP:0000280 | Coarse facial features | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Peripheral pulmonary artery stenosis is one of the most characteristic cardiovascular manifestations of Alagille syndrome, affecting approximately 76% of patients in large cohorts.

evidence.reference_title: Analysis of cardiovascular phenotype and genotype-phenotype correlation in individuals with a JAG1 mutation and/or Alagille syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The most common abnormality was stenosis/hypoplasia of the branch pulmonary arteries (PAs), which was documented by imaging (n=111) or inferred from a peripheral pulmonary stenosis murmur (n=41) in 76% of subjects.

Alagille syndrome (AGS) was described more than 35 years ago as a genetic entity characterised by five major features: chronic cholestasis owing to paucity of interlobular bile ducts; peripheral pulmonary stenosis
HP:0004969 | Peripheral pulmonary artery stenosis | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Pointed chin is a frequent facial feature contributing to the characteristic triangular facies of Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000307 | Pointed chin | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Posterior embryotoxon is a characteristic ocular developmental anomaly in Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Alagille syndrome (AGS) was described more than 35 years ago as a genetic entity characterised by five major features: chronic cholestasis owing to paucity of interlobular bile ducts; peripheral pulmonary stenosis; butterfly like vertebral arch defect; posterior embryotoxon

nodes.description: Protruding ears are a frequent feature of the characteristic facies in Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000411 | Protruding ear | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Cholestatic pruritus is a major symptom burden in patients with hepatic involvement and often drives treatment escalation.

evidence.reference_title: Alagille Syndrome: A Focused Review on Clinical Features, Genetics, and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Medical management is supportive, focusing on clinical manifestations of disease, with liver transplant indicated for severe pruritus

nodes.description: Paucity of intrahepatic bile ducts is the histopathological hallmark of Alagille syndrome.
nodes.id: Reduced number of intrahepatic bile ducts

evidence.snippet: HP:0006571 | Reduced number of intrahepatic bile ducts | Very frequent (99-80%)
chronic cholestasis owing to paucity of interlobular bile ducts

nodes.description: Renal hypoplasia or aplasia reflects the developmental renal involvement in Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0008678 | Renal hypoplasia/aplasia | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Spina bifida occulta is a frequent vertebral anomaly in Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0003298 | Spina bifida occulta | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Strabismus occurs occasionally as part of the ocular involvement in Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000486 | Strabismus | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Cutaneous telangiectasia is a frequent dermatological manifestation of Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0100585 | Telangiectasia of the skin | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Tetralogy of Fallot is present in approximately 12% of Alagille syndrome patients, making it the most common structural cardiac malformation after branch pulmonary artery stenosis.

evidence.reference_title: Analysis of cardiovascular phenotype and genotype-phenotype correlation in individuals with a JAG1 mutation and/or Alagille syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Tetralogy of Fallot was present in 23 subjects and was accompanied by pulmonary atresia in 8.

nodes.description: Ventricular septal defects occur as part of the congenital cardiac malformation spectrum in Alagille syndrome. The Orphanet annotation of VERY_FREQUENT appears discordant with clinical cohort data; the dominant cardiac lesion is branch pulmonary artery stenosis (76%), with structural defects such as tetralogy of Fallot (including VSD) present in ~12% of patients.

evidence.reference_title: Analysis of cardiovascular phenotype and genotype-phenotype correlation in individuals with a JAG1 mutation and/or Alagille syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Tetralogy of Fallot was present in 23 subjects and was accompanied by pulmonary atresia in 8.
HP:0001629 | Ventricular septal defect | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Vertebral segmentation defects beyond butterfly vertebrae are frequent skeletal manifestations of Alagille syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0003422 | Vertebral segmentation defect | Frequent (79-30%)
